topic: Why Some Storms Produce Hail the Size of Softballs
title: Exploring the Formation of Large Hailstones in Thunderstorms

lesson.learn: Thunderstorms with hailstones as large as softballs are rare, yet awe-inspiring phenomena that form through a complex interplay of atmospheric conditions. A study published in 2020 in the Journal of Applied Meteorology and Climatology found that large hailstones are typically associated with strong updrafts and the presence of supercooled water droplets in the storm cloud. This occurs when warm, moist air rises into the freezing levels of the atmosphere, where water droplets freeze into small balls of ice. As the storm cloud continues to grow and intensify, the hailstones are carried upward by strong updrafts, where they are repeatedly frozen and melted, growing larger and more angular. The largest hailstones have been recorded in countries such as the United States, where a massive hailstorm in Vivian, South Dakota in 2013 dropped hailstones weighing up to 1.9 kg (4.2 lbs).
lesson.simpler: Thunderstorms can be thought of as massive mixing vessels, where warm air rises and cools, causing water droplets to freeze into small balls of ice. As the storm intensifies, the hailstones are carried upward by strong updrafts and repeatedly frozen and melted, growing larger and more angular.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The presence of strong updrafts and supercooled water droplets in the storm cloud are the primary factors influencing the formation of large hailstones. Understanding these conditions is crucial for predicting and preparing for devastating hailstorms.
lesson.deeperDive: Several studies have highlighted the importance of low-level wind shear in the formation of large hailstones. For instance, a severe thunderstorm in Colorado in June 2020 was characterized by a strong low-level jet, which led to the formation of large hailstones in excess of 5 cm (2 in) in diameter. Research has also shown that hailstones form through a process known as accretion, where multiple small balls of ice stick together to form larger stones. This process can occur repeatedly, resulting in hailstones with diameters much larger than their individual components.
lesson.funFact: According to records from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), the largest hailstone ever recorded weighed 1.9 kg (4.2 lbs) and fell in Vivian, South Dakota in 2013.

places: National Center for Atmospheric Research (Boulder, CO)
